one_line_takeaway: MIDST is the first public benchmark showing diffusion-generated synthetic tabular data leaks membership well above chance — up to 46% TPR@10%FPR white-box — proving synthetic data is not a default anonymization guarantee.
key_finding: Diffusion-based synthetic tabular data is NOT inherently privacy-preserving — membership can be inferred well above random across all track types. The Tartan Federer team won all four tracks. White-box attacks were far stronger than black-box, and single-table easier than multi-table: winning TPR@10%FPR was 46% (white-box single), 35% (white-box multi), 25% (black-box single), and 23% (black-box multi). Notably the white-box multi-table track had NO announced runner-up because other submissions did not significantly beat random guessing, showing multi-relational synthesis is a harder attack surface. The organizers also observe TabDDPM was targeted/attacked more successfully than the latent-space TabSyn (open question whether TabSyn is genuinely more resilient or just less-explored), and that the intuition 'single-table attacks transfer to multi-table with equal-or-better success' did NOT hold, especially in the white-box track.
core_contribution: This is a competition report (not a new attack method) by the organizers of the MIDST Challenge at IEEE SaTML 2025, run by the Vector Institute. The paper's OWN contribution is the design and execution of the first public benchmark for membership inference attacks (MIAs) specifically against diffusion-model-based synthetic TABULAR data, plus a synthesis of the results. It establishes four evaluation tracks crossing model access (black-box vs white-box) with data structure (single-table vs multi-table/multi-relational), releases reference target models and shadow models, and reports how ~700 submissions from 71 participants performed. The organizers explicitly frame the novelty as being the first to evaluate diffusion-based synthetic tabular data (and, uniquely, multi-relational tables) under MIAs; the actual winning attack techniques are the participants' own external work, cited from separate reports/repos.
threat_model: Adversary goal: given the synthetic output (and optionally the trained generator) of a diffusion-based tabular data synthesizer, decide for each of a set of challenge records whether it was a MEMBER of the generator's training set or a non-member drawn from a same-distribution holdout. Each target model is trained on a split of a public dataset; for each model, m challenge points are provided, exactly half members and half non-members. Two access levels: WHITE-BOX (adversary has the target model parameters plus generated synthetic output) and BLACK-BOX (same information EXCEPT the target model itself — only synthetic output). In all tracks the adversary is additionally given shadow models trained the same way. The threat is that 'synthetic data as anonymization' is not a default privacy guarantee: successful MIA implies the generator memorizes/leaks individual training records, undermining GDPR/PIPEDA/CCPA-style anonymization claims for regulated finance and healthcare use.
limitations: Stated/evident limitations: (1) Only diffusion models are evaluated — the report explicitly notes it remains unclear whether the observed privacy leakage is specific to diffusion models versus other GenAI tabular synthesizers, since no comparison was run. (2) A single dataset (Berka financial data) and a single 20k-record subsample of one table underpin all tracks, limiting generality across domains and data scales. (3) Challenge points were confined to the Transaction table even in multi-table tracks, so multi-relational leakage is only partially probed. (4) As a competition report, the paper does not itself contribute a novel attack or a controlled ablation; the strongest results come from participants' external, separately-published solutions. (5) The white-box multi-table track produced essentially only one meaningful result (winner 35%), giving weak statistical grounding for that track. (6) TabSyn was under-attacked, so the relative resilience of latent-space diffusion is unresolved.

datasets_used: Berka dataset (public Czech-bank financial data; 8 tables, ~5,300 clients, ~1,000,000 transactions, ~700 loans, ~900 credit cards)
20,000-record subsample of the Transaction table of Berka (used to train all target and shadow models)
quantitative_results: White-box single-table: winner Tartan Federer 46% (TPR@10%FPR), runner-up Yan Pang 39%
White-box multi-table: winner Tartan Federer 35%; no runner-up announced (other submissions ~random guessing)
Black-box single-table: winner Tartan Federer 25%, runner-up CITADEL & UQAM 22%
Black-box multi-table: winner Tartan Federer 23%, runner-up Cyber@BGU 20%
Participation: over 700 submissions from 71 participants across 4 tracks
Released 30 shadow models per target model type for the train phase; 20 models in dev and 20 in final phases (ground truth withheld)
Evaluation metric: True Positive Rate at 10% False Positive Rate (TPR @ 0.1 FPR)
baselines_compared: Random guessing (baseline; white-box multi-table submissions other than the winner did not significantly exceed it)
Target/reference generative models: TabDDPM (single-table), TabSyn (single-table, latent-space), ClavaDDPM (multi-table)

Prior art referenced for context, not run by organizers: MICO competition (SaTML 2023, DP training vs white-box MIA); attack methods used by participants include SecMI, GSA, RMIA, DOMIAS, and Gower-distance ensembles
